recipes.title: Ful Sudani
recipes.description: Sudan's national breakfast: slow-cooked fava beans seasoned with cumin, garlic, and lemon, finished with cheese and oil.
steps: Soak the fava beans overnight in plenty of cold water, then drain.
Place the beans in a heavy pot, cover with the fresh water, and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer, partially covered, for 90 minutes until the beans are very soft, topping up water as needed.
Add the salt and cook for another 10 minutes.
Lightly mash about half of the beans against the side of the pot for a creamy texture, leaving the rest whole.
Stir in the garlic, cumin, and lemon juice and warm through for 2 minutes.
Spoon into bowls and drizzle generously with olive oil.
Top with diced tomato, crumbled feta, coriander, and chilli.
Serve hot with kisra, gurasa, or pita bread.

recipes.title: Kisra
recipes.description: Thin, fermented Sudanese sorghum flatbread, paper-light and slightly sour, served alongside stews.

ingredients.name: starter (kisra batter from a previous batch)

steps: In a large bowl, whisk the sorghum flour with the water and starter until smooth.
Cover loosely and leave at room temperature for 24-36 hours until pleasantly sour and bubbly.
Whisk in the salt and add a little extra water if needed; the batter should be like thin pancake batter.
Heat a flat griddle or wide non-stick pan over medium heat with no oil.
Pour a ladle of batter onto the griddle and use a flat scraper or the bottom of a flat-bottomed cup to spread it into a paper-thin sheet about 30 cm wide.
Cook for about 60-90 seconds without flipping until the edges lift and the surface is dry.
Slide off the griddle and stack on a clean cloth, separating each sheet with a layer of cloth or parchment.
Serve folded next to mulah, ful, or aseeda.

recipes.title: Mulah
recipes.description: Sudanese family of stews built on dried okra or peanut butter, simmered with meat and warm spices.
steps: Heat the oil in a heavy pot and brown the lamb on all sides, then remove.
In the same pot, sauté the onion until golden, about 8 minutes.
Add the garlic, coriander, cumin, and cardamom and cook for 1 minute.
Stir in the tomatoes and tomato paste and cook into a thick paste, about 5 minutes.
Return the lamb, pour in the water, and bring to a boil. Lower heat and simmer covered for 60 minutes.
Whisk the peanut butter with 250 ml of the hot stew liquid in a bowl until smooth.
Stir the peanut mixture back into the pot, season with salt, and simmer uncovered for 20 minutes until the sauce is rich and slightly thickened.
Adjust salt and serve with kisra or rice.

recipes.title: Gurasa
recipes.description: Soft, thick Sudanese pan-cooked flatbread with a tender crumb, perfect for scooping stews.
steps: Dissolve the yeast and sugar in the warm water and rest for 10 minutes until foamy.
In a bowl, whisk the flour and salt. Pour in the yeast mixture and oil and mix into a soft dough.
Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic, then cover and rest for 1 hour until doubled.
Punch down the dough and divide into 6 equal balls.
Roll each ball into a 1 cm thick disc about 18 cm wide.
Heat a wide flat pan over medium heat with no oil.
Cook each gurasa for 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown spots form and the bread is fully cooked through.
Stack wrapped in a clean towel to keep soft and serve warm with mulah or ful.

recipes.title: Aseeda
recipes.description: Sudanese stiff porridge of wheat or sorghum flour, served in a mound with stew poured around it.
steps: Bring 700 ml of the water to a rolling boil in a heavy pot with the salt.
Mix the flour with the remaining 300 ml cold water in a bowl until completely smooth.
Pour the slurry slowly into the boiling water while stirring vigorously with a long wooden spoon.
Reduce heat to medium-low and stir continuously, pressing the mixture against the sides of the pot.
Cook for 15 minutes until the mixture is very thick, glossy, and pulls cleanly away from the pot.
Stir in the ghee and cook for another 2 minutes.
Wet a deep bowl, scoop the aseeda in, smooth the top, and invert onto a serving platter to form a tall mound.
Make a well in the centre with the back of a spoon and serve immediately with mulah ladled around it.

recipes.title: Tamia
recipes.description: Sudanese fava bean falafel, lighter and greener than the Egyptian version, deep-fried until crisp.
steps: Soak the split fava beans in plenty of cold water for at least 12 hours, then drain very well.
In a food processor, pulse the fava beans, onion, garlic, parsley, coriander, and leek into a coarse, fluffy paste, scraping down as needed.
Add the cumin, ground coriander, and salt and pulse to combine.
Transfer to a bowl, cover, and rest in the fridge for 30 minutes.
Stir in the baking soda just before frying.
Heat the oil to 170°C.
Shape the mixture into small flat patties, press one side into sesame seeds, and slide carefully into the oil.
Fry in batches for 3-4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and crisp.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot in pita with salad and tahini.

recipes.title: Asida
recipes.description: Smooth, dough-like Sudanese pudding of wheat flour, traditionally served on Eid with honey or stew.
steps: Bring 600 ml of the water to a boil in a heavy pot with the salt.
Whisk the flour with the remaining 200 ml cold water until completely smooth and lump-free.
Pour the slurry slowly into the boiling water while whisking constantly.
Reduce heat to low and switch to a wooden spoon, stirring vigorously to keep the mixture smooth.
Cook for 15-20 minutes, pressing and folding the dough against the pot until it becomes glossy, elastic, and pulls cleanly from the sides.
Wet a deep bowl, scoop the asida in, smooth the top, and invert onto a serving platter.
Make a wide well in the centre.
Pour the melted ghee and honey into the well and serve warm, scooping pieces into the syrup with the fingertips.

recipes.title: Salata Aswad
recipes.description: Sudanese smoky aubergine and peanut salad, blackened over fire and dressed with lemon and chilli.
steps: Pierce the aubergines all over with a fork and char them directly over an open gas flame, turning often, until the skins are blackened and the flesh is fully soft, about 15 minutes.
Transfer to a covered bowl and let steam for 10 minutes.
Peel off the charred skins under cool running water and squeeze out excess liquid.
Roughly chop the smoky flesh and place in a bowl.
Whisk the peanut butter with the lemon juice and 2-3 tbsp warm water until smooth.
Stir in the garlic, chilli, cumin, and salt.
Fold the dressing into the aubergine and mash gently with a fork until creamy with some texture.
Drizzle with olive oil and serve cold with kisra or bread.

recipes.title: Kawari
recipes.description: Slow-cooked Sudanese sheep trotter stew, gelatinous and rich, simmered with onions and spices for hours.
steps: Scrub the trotters thoroughly. Place in a large pot, cover with cold water, and bring to a boil for 5 minutes, then drain and rinse.
Return the trotters to the pot with the fresh water, the cinnamon, and bay leaves.
Bring to a boil, skim the foam, and simmer covered for 3 hours until very tender and gelatinous, topping up water as needed.
In a separate pan, heat the oil and sauté the onions until deeply golden, about 12 minutes.
Add the garlic, cumin, ground coriander, and turmeric and cook for 1 minute.
Stir in the tomatoes and tomato paste and cook into a thick sauce, about 6 minutes.
Tip the sauce into the trotter pot and add the salt.
Simmer uncovered for 30 minutes until the broth thickens and coats the trotters.
Stir in the lemon juice and fresh coriander, then serve hot with bread.
